hero.title: 🧠 NIS Microbiology
hero.subtitle: The intuitive mind is a sacred gift and the rational mind is a faithful servant - Albert Einstein

sections.hint: Focus on lesion localization, gaze pathways, brainstem syndromes, and basal ganglia circuits.

topics.content:
• PPRF (Pons): Horizontal gaze center → coordinates CN VI + CN III via MLF.
  • Frontal Eye Field (Area 8): Drives contralateral gaze.
  • MLF: Connects CN III & VI → lesion = INO (adduction failure + nystagmus).
  • INO: Convergence intact (NOT CN III palsy).
  • One-and-a-half: PPRF + MLF lesion → only one eye abducts.
  • Eight-and-a-half: + CN VII palsy → facial weakness.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: Which statement best distinguishes innate immunity from adaptive immunity?
a: Innate immunity provides immediate non-specific protection
Innate immunity is mediated only by antibodies
immunity_and_immune_system.l: Innate immunity provides rapid, non-specific protection against infection.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: Immunity is best defined as:
immunity_and_immune_system.l: Immunity refers to resistance against disease and infection.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: The immune system is composed of:
a: Cells, tissues, and molecules that mediate resistance to infection
immunity_and_immune_system.l: The immune system includes cells, tissues, and molecules that defend against infection.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: Which type of immunity provides immediate protection against microbial invasion?
immunity_and_immune_system.l: Innate immunity acts immediately upon pathogen exposure.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: Adaptive immunity differs from innate immunity because it:
a: Develops more slowly and is highly specific
immunity_and_immune_system.l: Adaptive immunity develops slowly and targets specific antigens.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: Which cells mediate cell-mediated immunity?
immunity_and_immune_system.l: Cell-mediated immunity is mediated by T lymphocytes.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: Humoral immunity is primarily mediated by:
immunity_and_immune_system.l: Humoral immunity is mediated by antibody-producing B lymphocytes.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: Which of the following is a major innate immune cell?
immunity_and_immune_system.l: Neutrophils are major innate immune cells.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: Which feature is characteristic of innate immunity?
immunity_and_immune_system.l: Innate immunity provides an immediate response to pathogens.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: Which molecule is part of innate immunity?
immunity_and_immune_system.l: Lysozyme is an innate antimicrobial enzyme.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: Adaptive immunity recognizes antigens primarily through:
immunity_and_immune_system.l: Adaptive immunity uses BCRs and TCRs for antigen recognition.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: Innate immune recognition of microbes is based on detection of:
immunity_and_immune_system.l: Innate immunity recognizes pathogen-associated molecular patterns (PAMPs).

immunity_and_immune_system.q: Pattern recognition receptors (PRRs) are primarily associated with:
immunity_and_immune_system.l: PRRs are receptors of the innate immune system.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: TLR-2 primarily recognizes:
immunity_and_immune_system.l: TLR-2 recognizes peptidoglycan from Gram-positive bacteria.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: TLR-4 is the principal receptor for:
immunity_and_immune_system.l: TLR-4 recognizes LPS from Gram-negative bacteria.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: TLR-5 recognizes which microbial structure?
immunity_and_immune_system.l: TLR-5 recognizes bacterial flagellin.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: Which Toll-like receptors recognize single-stranded viral RNA?
immunity_and_immune_system.l: TLR-7 and TLR-8 detect viral ssRNA.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: TLR-3 is most important for recognition of:
immunity_and_immune_system.l: TLR-3 recognizes viral dsRNA.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: NF-kB functions primarily as a:
immunity_and_immune_system.l: NF-kB is a transcription factor regulating immune-response genes.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: Activation of NF-kB results in:
immunity_and_immune_system.l: NF-kB promotes expression of cytokines and immune genes.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: Which of the following is considered a first-line physical barrier against infection?
immunity_and_immune_system.l: Skin serves as a major physical barrier against pathogens.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: Lysozyme found in tears protects against infection by:
immunity_and_immune_system.l: Lysozyme cleaves peptidoglycan in bacterial cell walls.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: Respiratory cilia help prevent infection by:
immunity_and_immune_system.l: Respiratory cilia clear microbes by mucociliary transport.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: Which innate immune cell is specialized for killing virus-infected cells?
immunity_and_immune_system.l: NK cells destroy virus-infected cells.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: Which cytokines are important in antiviral defense?
immunity_and_immune_system.l: Type I interferons inhibit viral replication.

acute_inflammatory_response.q: The Purkinje cells of the cerebellar cortex:
a: Provides the only output to the deep cerebellar nuclei
Receives inhibitory input from climbing fibers
Are excited by the axons of the basket cells
acute_inflammatory_response.l: Purkinje cells are the sole output neurons of the cerebellar cortex and send inhibitory (GABAergic) signals to deep cerebellar nuclei.
acute_inflammatory_response.h: Only output of cerebellar cortex → Purkinje cells

acute_inflammatory_response.q: Lucy is a 55-year-old woman with a history of hypertension and diabetes for more than 15 years. While trying to get up from the floor, she noticed that she was unable to move her right arm or leg. Her speech was slurred and unintelligible. She lay on the floor and waited for help to arrive. At the emergency room, she was found to follow commands well, and although her speech was very slurred, it was logical in organization. The lower two-thirds of her face pointed to the right side when asked to protrude her tongue. Her right arm and leg were severely but equally weak. The left side was normal. Where is the lesion most likely located?
acute_inflammatory_response.l: Equal contralateral face, arm, and leg weakness with dysarthria indicates a lacunar stroke of the internal capsule due to small vessel disease from hypertension and diabetes.
acute_inflammatory_response.h: Equal face + arm + leg weakness → internal capsule (pure motor stroke)

acute_inflammatory_response.q: Which of the following nuclei in the thalamus projects to area 3, 1, 2 of the cortex?
acute_inflammatory_response.l: Area 3, 1, 2 is the primary somatosensory cortex. The Ventral Posterolateral (VPL) nucleus relays sensory information (pain, temperature, touch, proprioception) from the body to this cortical area.

acute_inflammatory_response.q: Heschl’s convolutions are located in which of the following areas?
acute_inflammatory_response.l: Heschl’s gyrus (transverse temporal gyri) represents the primary auditory cortex, located in the superior temporal lobe (Brodmann areas 41 & 42).

acute_inflammatory_response.q: A 55-year-old overweight man collapsed while loading a truck. Examination revealed paresis of both right limbs with a Babinski sign on the right. The patient’s tongue deviated to the left upon protrusion, and he had no vibratory sense on the left side of the body. These findings suggest:
a: A lesion in the left medial medulla
A lesion in the right medial pons
A lesion in the right medial medulla
A lesion in the lateral medulla
A lesion in the left medial pons
acute_inflammatory_response.l: Medial Medullary Syndrome (Dejerine syndrome) involves the pyramid (contralateral hemiparesis), medial lemniscus (contralateral vibration loss), and CN XII (ipsilateral tongue deviation). Tongue deviates toward the lesion side (Left).
acute_inflammatory_response.h: Contralateral motor/sensory + Ipsilateral tongue = Medial Medulla

acute_inflammatory_response.q: Symptoms of cerebellar disease include all of the following EXCEPT:
acute_inflammatory_response.l: The cerebellum coordinates movement but does not initiate it. Lesions cause ataxia, intention tremors, and dysarthria, but NOT paralysis (which is a sign of UMN/LMN lesions).
acute_inflammatory_response.h: Cerebellum = Coordination; Motor initiation = Cortex (Paralysis ≠ Cerebellum)

acute_inflammatory_response.q: A 40-year-old male has difficulty producing a smile from the left side of his face. Muscles are flaccid and the eyelids are open. Where do the cell bodies of origin within the CNS lie?
acute_inflammatory_response.l: This describes a Bell's palsy or Lower Motor Neuron facial nerve (CN VII) lesion. The facial nerve nucleus is located in the lower pons.
acute_inflammatory_response.h: Facial nerve nucleus (CN VII) → Lower pons

acute_inflammatory_response.q: The lateral geniculate body:
a: Transmits visual information to the cerebral cortex
Is a visual motor reflex center
Relays auditory information to the cerebral cortex
acute_inflammatory_response.l: The Lateral Geniculate Nucleus (LGN) of the thalamus is the primary relay station for visual information from the retina to the primary visual cortex (BA 17).

acute_inflammatory_response.q: A 50-year-old woman has right-sided spastic paralysis, loss of vibratory sense on the right, and internuclear ophthalmoplegia (INO) where the left eye would not adduct. Her tongue deviated toward the right. Where is the site of lesion?
acute_inflammatory_response.l: The presence of Internuclear Ophthalmoplegia (INO) signifies a lesion of the Medial Longitudinal Fasciculus (MLF), which is a classic localizing sign for the pons.

acute_inflammatory_response.q: A 62-year-old man presents with slow shuffling gait, stooped posture, masked facies, micrographia, and a resting pill-rolling tremor. What is the diagnosis?
acute_inflammatory_response.l: Parkinson’s disease is characterized by the 'TRAP' mnemonic: Tremor (resting), Rigidity, Akinesia/bradykinesia, and Postural instability, along with masked facies and micrographia.
acute_inflammatory_response.h: Pill-rolling tremor + Shuffling gait + Masked facies = Parkinson’s

acute_inflammatory_response.q: A 70-year-old woman presents with the complaint of difficulty walking. The history reveals that the woman suddenly had problems while she was playing with her granddaughter at the local park. The examination reveals a slender, otherwise healthy woman who has ataxia and is unable to do the finger-to-nose and the heel-to-shin tests on the right, and has slurred, garbled speech. Which of the following specifically describes the pattern of movement in this woman?
acute_inflammatory_response.l: Dysmetria is a cerebellar sign characterized by inability to accurately judge distance, leading to overshooting or undershooting during movements like finger-to-nose testing.

acute_inflammatory_response.q: A patient who suffers from chronic alcohol abuse, which affects neurons in the cerebellum, will most likely:
acute_inflammatory_response.l: Chronic alcohol abuse commonly damages the anterior vermis of the cerebellum, leading to gait ataxia (truncal instability).

acute_inflammatory_response.q: Which cortical area is responsible for taste recognition?
acute_inflammatory_response.l: Brodmann area 43 corresponds to the gustatory cortex responsible for taste perception.

acute_inflammatory_response.q: Huntington’s disease is characterized by neuronal degeneration in which of the following structures?
acute_inflammatory_response.l: Huntington’s disease involves degeneration of GABAergic neurons in the striatum (especially the caudate nucleus), leading to chorea.

acute_inflammatory_response.q: Which of the following is an excitatory neuron within the cerebellar cortex?
acute_inflammatory_response.l: Granule cells are the only excitatory neurons in the cerebellar cortex, while all others (Purkinje, basket, stellate, Golgi) are inhibitory.

acute_inflammatory_response.q: A 35-year-old woman is brought to her family physician by her husband. The results of the examination reveal deficits that suggest the woman has had a precipitous vascular event. A CT is ordered, revealing a small stroke in the genu of the internal capsule. Which of the following fiber populations is most affected in this woman's stroke?
acute_inflammatory_response.l: The genu of the internal capsule contains corticonuclear (corticobulbar) fibers that control cranial nerve motor functions.

acute_inflammatory_response.q: The neural tract that connects Broca’s area (Brodmann’s 44) with Wernicke’s area (Brodmann’s 22) and regulates fluent speech is the:
acute_inflammatory_response.l: The arcuate fasciculus connects Broca’s and Wernicke’s areas and is essential for coordinated speech; damage causes conduction aphasia.

acute_inflammatory_response.q: Which dopaminergic pathway projects mainly to the caudate nucleus?
acute_inflammatory_response.l: The nigrostriatal pathway carries dopamine from the substantia nigra to the striatum (caudate and putamen) and is essential for movement control.

acute_inflammatory_response.q: A 63-year-old patient is seen in a clinic with the following symptoms: a resting tremor in the hand, a slow shuffling gait, and difficulty arising from a chair when seated. These symptoms could be caused by a lesion in the:
acute_inflammatory_response.l: Degeneration of dopaminergic neurons in the substantia nigra causes Parkinson’s disease, characterized by tremor, rigidity, and bradykinesia.

acute_inflammatory_response.q: Gamma aminobutyric acid (GABA) is considered the predominant neurotransmitter in all of the following except?
acute_inflammatory_response.l: Corticostriate fibers use glutamate (excitatory), whereas basal ganglia intrinsic pathways (striatal and pallidal) primarily use GABA (inhibitory).

acute_inflammatory_response.q: All of the following are the signs of lateral pontine syndrome EXCEPT?
a: Ipsilateral pain and temperature loss from the face
Contralateral pain and temperature loss from the body
Contralateral loss of discriminative touch and vibration in the body
acute_inflammatory_response.l: Lateral pontine syndrome affects spinothalamic and trigeminal pathways but spares the medial lemniscus, so vibration and discriminative touch are preserved.

acute_inflammatory_response.q: Lucy is a 55-year-old woman who has had high blood pressure and diabetes for more than 15 years. One day, as she was reaching for a switch to put on the light she collapsed. While trying to get up from the floor, she noticed that she was unable to move her right arm or leg. Lucy attempted to cry for help because she was unable to reach the telephone; however, her speech was slurred and rather unintelligible. She lay on the floor and waited for help to arrive. Lucy’s son began to worry about his usually prompt mother when she didn’t arrive for his birthday. After several attempts to telephone her apartment without getting an answer, he drove there to find her lying on the floor. She attempted to tell him what had happened, but her speech was too slurred to comprehend. Assuming that his mother had had a stroke, the son called an ambulance to take her to the nearest emergency room (ER). A neurology resident was called to see Lucy in the emergency room because the physicians there likewise thought that she had a stroke. The resident noted that Lucy followed commands very well, and although her speech was very slurred, it was logical and organized. Also, the lower two-thirds of her face drooped on the right. Her tongue pointed to the right side when she was asked to protrude it. Her right arm and leg were severely, but equally weak; her left side had normal strength. Where did Lucy’s stroke most likely happen?
acute_inflammatory_response.l: A lesion in the left internal capsule causes contralateral pure motor deficits (face, arm, leg) with dysarthria due to corticospinal and corticobulbar fiber involvement.

acute_inflammatory_response.q: A lesion of the genu of the internal capsule on the right side could result in all but which of the following?
a: Inability to smile with the left side of the face on command
Deviation of the tongue to the left on protrusion
All of the above could result
acute_inflammatory_response.l: The genu of the internal capsule contains corticonuclear fibers, so lesions can cause contralateral facial weakness, tongue deviation, and cranial nerve-related deficits like hyperacusis.

acute_inflammatory_response.q: Which of the following thalamic nuclei receives important projections from the internal segment of the globus pallidus and from the contralateral cerebellar nuclei?
acute_inflammatory_response.l: The ventral lateral (VL) nucleus of the thalamus receives input from the globus pallidus and cerebellum and relays motor information to the motor cortex.

acute_inflammatory_response.q: Lesions of the superior parietal lobule interfere with the patient’s ability to combine touch, pressure, and proprioceptive impulses, so he or she is unable to appreciate texture, size, and form. This loss of integration of sensory impulses is called:
acute_inflammatory_response.l: Astereognosis is the inability to recognize objects by touch despite intact primary sensory modalities, due to parietal lobe damage.

acute_inflammatory_response.q: A 55-year-old overweight man was brought to the emergency room unconscious after he had collapsed while loading a truck. After he regained consciousness, an exam revealed paresis of both right limbs with a Babinski sign on the right. The patient’s tongue deviated to the left upon protrusion, with fasciculations present, and he had no vibratory sense on the right side of the body. These findings suggest?
a: A lesion in the medial medulla
A lesion in the medial pons
A lesion in the lateral medulla
A lesion in the medial mid brain
acute_inflammatory_response.l: Medial medullary syndrome (ASA infarct) causes contralateral paralysis and vibration loss with ipsilateral tongue deviation toward the lesion.

acute_inflammatory_response.q: A 55-year-old man with a history of hypertension has been in excellent health until he notices that he has vertigo and unsteadiness lasting for a week. He then developed nausea, vomiting, dysphagia, hoarseness, ataxia, left facial pain, and right-sided sensory loss. There is no weakness. On examination, he is alert, with a normal mental status. There is left ptosis. He has loss of pain and temperature sensation on the right arm and leg and decreased joint position sensation in the left foot. He is unable to walk. The analgesia and thermal anesthesia on the left side of the face in this case most likely resulted from a lesion of which structure?
a: The principal (chief) sensory nucleus of V
acute_inflammatory_response.l: Lateral medullary (Wallenberg) syndrome damages the spinal nucleus of CN V, causing ipsilateral loss of pain and temperature sensation from the face.

acute_inflammatory_response.q: A 55-year-old overweight man was brought to the emergency room unconscious after he had collapsed while loading a truck. After he regained consciousness, an exam revealed paresis of both right limbs with a Babinski sign on the right. The patient’s tongue deviated to the left upon protrusion, and he had no vibratory sense on the right side of the body. These findings suggest:
a: A lesion in the left medial medulla
A lesion in the right medial pons
A lesion in the right medial medulla
A lesion in the lateral medulla
A lesion in the left medial pons
acute_inflammatory_response.l: Medial medullary syndrome causes contralateral paralysis and vibration loss with ipsilateral tongue deviation toward the lesion.

acute_inflammatory_response.q: Descending projections from the midbrain participate in pathways that are directly involved in the inhibition of pain in the spinal trigeminal nucleus in the pars caudalis, and in the posterior horn of the spinal cord. This region of the midbrain contains many cells with opiate receptors. Which of the following is the most likely location of these cells?
acute_inflammatory_response.l: The periaqueductal gray (PAG) in the midbrain contains opioid receptors and plays a key role in descending pain inhibition.

acute_inflammatory_response.q: The inferior colliculi are considered to be:
a: Directly involved in and a part of the visual pathway
Directly involved in and a part of the auditory pathway
Directly involved in and a part of the olfactory pathway
acute_inflammatory_response.l: The inferior colliculi are midbrain structures that serve as major relay centers in the auditory pathway.
